symbols: crown of thorns — mockery of kingship that paradoxically reveals true divine sovereignty
scarlet robe — imperial purple twisted into ridicule, yet signifying Christ's true royal dignity
bound or exposed body — the Suffering Servant stripped and afflicted (Isa 53)
Pilate's gesture — worldly authority presenting divine innocence to a guilty world
mourning woman below — the Mater Dolorosa, sharing in her Son's suffering
putti/angels — heavenly witnesses to the cosmic significance of the Passion
description: Christ sits nearly nude on a stone block, crowned with thorns, a red cloak draped loosely over his lap and shoulder. His body shows signs of scourging. Behind him, a robed elder figure -- likely Pilate -- extends a cloth or garment over Christ while a soldier assists. Below in the foreground, a sorrowful blue-robed woman (the Virgin Mary) reclines in grief, and small angelic figures or putti attend nearby. The background opens to a distant landscape under a partly cloudy sky. The composition is vertical and tightly compressed, with rich Mannerist coloring.
theological_description: The Ecce Homo ('Behold the Man,' John 19:5) is the moment when Pontius Pilate presents the scourged and mocked Jesus to the Jerusalem crowd, unwittingly proclaiming a profound christological truth: behold the true Man, the Second Adam (1 Cor 15:45), in whom divinity is veiled under the most abject human suffering. The crown of thorns inverts the curse of Genesis 3:18 ('thorns and thistles shall the ground bring forth'), as Christ bears creation's penalty upon His own head. The scarlet robe (Matt 27:28) mocks His kingship while fulfilling Isaiah's vision of one who comes from Edom with garments stained crimson (Isa 63:1-2). Pilate's presentation is deeply ironic: the judge of the world is judged, the King of Kings is mocked as a pretender. Spranger's Mannerist treatment, with its muscular yet vulnerable Christ, emphasizes the paradox of divine power made perfect in weakness (2 Cor 12:9). The Virgin below participates in the Passion as the Mater Dolorosa, fulfilling Simeon's prophecy that a sword would pierce her soul (Luke 2:35).

scripture_refs.reason: Pilate brings Jesus out and declares 'Ecce Homo' -- the direct narrative source of the scene.
He was despised and rejected, a man of sorrows -- the Suffering Servant prophecy embodied in the scourged Christ.
The soldiers strip Jesus, put a scarlet robe and crown of thorns on Him, and mock Him as King of the Jews.
Thorns and thistles as the curse of the Fall, now borne by the Second Adam on His crown.
Who is this coming from Edom with garments stained crimson -- the scarlet robe as prophetic fulfillment.
Simeon's prophecy that a sword will pierce Mary's soul, fulfilled in her grief at the Passion.
My power is made perfect in weakness -- the paradox of divine omnipotence in humiliation.
The last Adam became a life-giving spirit -- Christ as the true Man whom Pilate unwittingly reveals.
